critic_raw:
Assessor A: The defect classifier is evaluated on a metric that is structurally unable to measure its effectiveness for the stated purpose. At 1.4% prevalence, a trivial baseline achieves 98.6% accuracy. The 0.5 pp improvement could be entirely driven by better separation of non-defects. The paper claims the model reduces defect escapes — but reports no defect-class metric. The cost asymmetry (missed defects >> false rejections) makes this worse: the evaluation is optimizing and measuring the wrong objective. Verdict: critique_wins.

Assessor B: This is a canonical example of the accuracy paradox in imbalanced classification. The evaluation provides no evidence about defect detection quality because the metric is dominated by the majority class. Furthermore, the paper's own discussion acknowledges that the cost of a missed defect is far higher than a false positive — yet uses symmetric accuracy for evaluation. This is an internal inconsistency that cannot be rescued without defect-class metrics. Verdict: critique_wins.

Assessor C: The deployment claim is that the model will 'reduce escapes of defective parts.' The evidence provided is a 0.5 pp improvement in overall accuracy. These are mismatched: accuracy at 1.4% prevalence measures mostly non-defect classification. Without recall on the defect class, the claim is unsupported. Speed and augmentation details confirm operational viability, not evaluation validity. Verdict: critique_wins.

Synthesis: All three assessors vote critique_wins. All three identify the same three issues. The consensus is clear and unambiguous.
adjudication_raw: Unanimous critique_wins across three assessors. All three identify accuracy-hiding-rare-event-performance as the primary issue, the cost-asymmetry inconsistency as secondary, and the missing operating point as tertiary. No ETD needed — the critique is structural, not empirically resolvable without better metrics.
